failure_modes.text: Abstract mission language can sound accurate while remaining useless for frontline decisions and execution.
failure_modes.source_quote: Useless Accuracy/Lack of Concreteness (The Curse of Knowledge)
failure_modes.mitigation: Test goals against frontline decisions: if the mission language cannot guide a specific day-to-day choice, it is abstract decoration.

failure_modes.text: Blurring goal setting with solution design can hide the real diagnosis step and lock the team into premature plans.
failure_modes.mitigation: Separate goal-setting from solution design so the diagnosis step is not hidden or skipped by premature planning.

failure_modes.text: Sticky goals can survive past their evidence, creating goal inertia instead of strategic adaptation.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when goals are sticky enough to organize action but not revisited often enough to reflect changed evidence, constraints, or strategic reality.
failure_modes.mitigation: Schedule goal reviews against current evidence; sticky goals that survive past their data create inertia instead of strategic adaptation.

premortem_questions.text: Is the worst case bad enough?
What would you have to believe?
If your audience remembers only one thing, what should it be?
premortem_questions.source_quote: if your audience remembers only one thing, what should it be?

heuristics.text: Set goals first, then diagnose problems, design plans, and execute in sequence instead of collapsing the steps together.
heuristics.source_quote: It is crucial to perform these steps one at a time and in order; for instance, when setting goals, one should only set goals and avoid thinking about how they will be achieved or what might go wrong.

heuristics.text: Use SMART goals so the objective is specific, measurable, attainable, relevant, and time-bound.
Tie low-level goals to one top-level professional goal so the system does not optimize disconnected aims.

curation_notes.summary: The source is strong on concreteness, sequencing, and strategic alignment, so the chunk set stays near those operational interventions.
donor_drops: Did not preserve donor-style motivational overlays because the raw model is more useful as an execution-governance discipline.
open_questions: The third premortem question comes from the mitigation section's communication test and is kept because it forces concrete target clarity.

allies: The raw allies section points to metacognition, design thinking, and structured process roles as later relation material.
antagonists: The raw antagonists section is thinner than the explicit tension block, so most conflict preservation belongs in Wave 3.
structured_tensions: The raw source explicitly preserves tensions against input-vs-output-goals, commitment-bias, and confirmation-bias.
